title: Reading a sett chart
subtitle: How to use a sett chart to choose the right reed and warp density for any yarn
excerpt: A sett chart translates yarn weight into threads per centimetre, giving you a starting point for warp density on any structure. This entry explains how sett charts are constructed, how to read them for plain weave and twill, and how to adjust when your yarn falls between chart values.
sourceNotes: Sett chart methodology is standard practice across UK and US weaving instruction; the wraps-per-centimetre method is the accepted universal baseline for calculating sett from any yarn.

glossaryTerms.definition: A measurement used to calculate sett: wind yarn closely (not tightly) around a ruler for 1 cm and count the wraps. Divide by 2 for a plain weave sett; divide by 1.5 for a twill sett. This gives the number of warp ends per centimetre as a starting point before sampling.

A cloth in which warp and weft threads are equally visible on the surface because the sett allows equal interlacement of both. A balanced plain weave has equal warp and weft sett; a balanced twill has warp and weft equally sharing the surface. Most project setts aim for a balanced weave unless a warp-faced or weft-faced cloth is intended.

content.text: Sett charts exist because published yarn counts (8/2, 10/2, DK, laceweight) are not consistent across suppliers and fibre types. The only reliable method is to measure the yarn you actually have: the
method gives a sett figure from the physical yarn, not from a label. A
is the aim for most projects: if warp and weft are equally visible, the cloth has good drape and the beat is consistent.

Wind the yarn around a ruler or pencil in a single layer, wrapping closely but not compressing the yarn. Count how many wraps fit in exactly 1 cm. Write down that number (WPC). For a plain weave sett, divide WPC by 2. For a twill sett, divide WPC by 1.5. The result is the number of warp ends per centimetre to aim for. If the result is 7.5, round to the nearest available reed size (7 or 8 dent).

Plain weave interlaces every thread over-and-under on every pick. This means the warp threads must be spaced far enough apart to allow the weft to pass through cleanly, but close enough to cover the warp. Twill has longer floats that allow warp threads to sit closer together, so a twill can be sett more closely than the same yarn in plain weave without the weft being unable to enter. This is why twill cloths have a denser, heavier hand than plain weave in the same yarn.
Adjusting when your yarn falls between reed sizes

Rigid heddle looms have fixed reed sizes (8, 10, 12, or 15 dent are common). A floor loom uses interchangeable reeds. If your calculated sett is 11 ends per 10 cm and your available reeds are 10 dent and 12 dent, you have two options: use the 10 dent reed with one thread per slot and one thread per hole as normal (giving 10 epc), or use the 12 dent reed with one thread per dent (giving 12 epc). Sample both options on your actual yarn and see which gives the drape and hand you want. Reed size is a starting point, not a prescription.

A sett chart gives you a starting warp density. Always sample before warping a full project. Weave at least 15 cm of plain weave on the actual yarn and reed combination, wet-finish the sample, and measure the sett in the finished cloth. If the cloth is warp-dominant (the warp colour shows more than the weft), the sett is too close. If the weft rows pack in and cover the warp, the sett is too open. Adjust the reed size by one step up or down and sample again.
